name: Rosen, Michael A.
recent_1: Marsteller, J. A., Rosen, M. A., Wyskiel, R., Chang, B. H., Hsu, Y.-J., Thompson, D. A., Kim, G., & Speck, K. (2024). Multi-Team Shared Expectations Tool (MT-SET): An exercise to improve teamwork across health care teams. The Joint Commission Journal on Quality and Patient Safety, 50(10), 737-744. DOI: 10.1016/j.jcjq.2024.05.012
recent_1_synopsis: This paper introduced and applied the Multi-Team Shared Expectations Tool, a structured exercise to align expectations and improve coordination across interacting health care teams (multi-team systems). It offers a practical intervention for the cross-team handoffs and dependencies that often undermine patient safety.
recent_2: Rosen, M. A., Shekelle, P. G., Treadwell, J. R., Stewart, C. M., Sharma, R., & Bass, E. B. (2024). Making healthcare safer IV: Marking a quarter century of patient safety improvement. Journal of Patient Safety and Risk Management, 29(3), 123-127. DOI: 10.1177/25160435241265792
recent_2_synopsis: This article frames the fourth 'Making Healthcare Safer' evidence review, reflecting on 25 years of patient-safety progress and remaining gaps. It sets priorities for which safety practices warrant continued attention and rigorous evaluation.
recent_3: Ali, K. J., Goeschel, C. A., Eckroade, M. M., Carlin, K. N., Haugstetter, M., Shofer, M., & Rosen, M. A. (2024). The TeamSTEPPS for Improving Diagnosis Team Assessment Tool: Scale development and psychometric evaluation. The Joint Commission Journal on Quality and Patient Safety, 50(2), 95-103. DOI: 10.1016/j.jcjq.2023.08.009
recent_3_synopsis: This study developed and psychometrically validated an assessment tool adapting TeamSTEPPS principles to measure teamwork behaviors relevant to diagnostic accuracy. It provides a measurement instrument for studying and improving team-based contributions to reducing diagnostic error.
seminal_1: Rosen, M. A., DiazGranados, D., Dietz, A. S., Benishek, L. E., Thompson, D., Pronovost, P. J., & Weaver, S. J. (2018). Teamwork in healthcare: Key discoveries enabling safer, high-quality care. American Psychologist, 73(4), 433-450. DOI: 10.1037/amp0000298
seminal_1_synopsis: This widely cited review synthesizes evidence on teams and teamwork in healthcare, linking teamwork to outcomes and detailing effective behaviors, underlying competencies, interventions, and performance-measurement strategies. With well over 1,500 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025) it is his most influential paper and a foundational reference for healthcare team science.
seminal_2: Rosen, M. A., Salas, E., Wilson, K. A., King, H. B., Salisbury, M., Augenstein, J. S., Robinson, D. W., & Birnbach, D. J. (2008). Measuring team performance in simulation-based training: Adopting best practices for healthcare. Simulation in Healthcare, 3(1), 33-41. DOI: 10.1097/SIH.0b013e3181626276
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper adapted established team-performance measurement best practices for healthcare simulation training, providing guidance on what and how to measure when evaluating clinical teams. With roughly 400 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is a foundational methodological reference for simulation-based team assessment.
expertise_overview: Human factors and applied/industrial-organizational psychology of teamwork and patient safety in healthcare. Develops, validates, and applies behavioral markers and measurement tools for team performance and team cognition across clinical domains, including multi-team systems and diagnostic teamwork. Studies simulation-based training design, implementation, and evaluation, and how in-situ simulation can diagnose unit safety needs. Investigates team physiological dynamics and the use of physiological measures to characterize team states, alongside naturalistic decision making and quality- and safety-improvement methods. Application domains center on acute and perioperative care and broader patient-safety systems.
notes: Identified with high confidence: Michael A. Rosen was a human factors psychologist and faculty member at the Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine (Armstrong Institute for Patient Safety and Quality), PhD under Eduardo Salas (Univ. of Central Florida). Confirmed via Johns Hopkins profiles, his Google Scholar profile (user c_UTJMMAAAAJ), and authorship on all four seed hf_papers and the recent papers. Citation counts are approximate, from Google Scholar around mid-2025 (the 2018 American Psychologist review exceeds 1,500). All six cited DOIs verified resolving via the Crossref API (June 2026) with matching titles and confirmed Rosen authorship; the four seed DOIs were pre-verified.
